data.title: Trump touts decline in US trade deficit despite meager decrease
data.description:
President Trump on Wednesday touted the decline in U.S. trade deficit ahead of official data showing a small reduction in the country’s overall balance of trade.

In a Wednesday night post on Truth Social, the president claimed credit for reducing the trade deficit — the difference between the value of U.S. imports and exports — through tariffs he imposed last year.

Trump claimed to have cut the trade deficit by 78 percent in the Truth Social post, which came less than 12 hours before the Census Bureau released the official figures.

“THE UNITED STATES TRADE DEFICIT HAS BEEN REDUCED BY 78% BECAUSE OF THE TARIFFS BEING CHARGED TO OTHER COMPANIES AND COUNTRIES,” the president claimed.

But the Census Bureau data released Thursday showed the trade deficit falling by only 0.2 percent in 2025, from $903.5 billion in 2024 to $901.5 billion last year.

During his second term, Trump’s tariff policy has rattled markets around the globe, increased economic uncertainty and strained relationships with historic U.S. allies like Canada and the European Union.

Trump has also been dealing with anxieties about the economy from the American public as the country approaches the 2026 midterms.

Last week, six House Republicans came together with Democrats to pass a resolution to roll back Trump’s tariffs on Canada. The move by the six Republicans marked a notable defeat for Trump and Republican House leadership.

The vote, 219-211, came in the wake of a House GOP revolt against leadership due to the administration’s handling of trade policy.

Trump’s lead White House economic adviser also recently suggested that a group of Federal Reserve researchers should face punishment over a paper looking into the costs of the president’s tariffs. 

National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett, in a recent interview, criticized a new paper from the Federal Reserve Bank of New York economists. According to the economists, consumers paid for 90 percent of the costs of Trump’s import taxes.

Updated Feb. 19 at 10:56 a.m. EST

data.title: Midsized firms' tariff payments tripled: Analysis
data.description:
A new analysis from the JPMorgan Chase Institute found that tariff payments by midsized U.S. firms roughly tripled in 2025. 

The analysis, published Thursday, shows that midsized firms’ tariff payments began increasing starting in April, the month that President Trump unveiled his sweeping “Liberation Day” levies on numerous trading partners. Tariff payments by midsized firms steadily rose from May through November, before declining slightly in December, the analysis found. 

The institute categorized midsized firms as those with revenue between $10 million and $1 billion or with between 50 and 499 workers. The analysis found that over half of such firms conduct transactions with international partners, with over 10 percent doing business with 10 or more foreign countries.

In imposing tariffs upon returning to office, Trump said his policies would contribute to reshored U.S. manufacturing and help correct the country’s expanding trade deficit. The Census Bureau and Bureau of Economic Analysis reported Thursday that the goods and services deficit decreased $2.1 billion from 2024 to 2025.

Researchers from the New York Federal Reserve, meanwhile, released findings last week that the average tariff rate rose from 2.6 to 13 percent last year. The New York Fed also found that the share of Chinese goods imported by the U.S. has fallen dramatically since 2017, with firms importing more goods from the likes of Mexico and Vietnam, among other countries.

White House Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett said Wednesday the Fed researchers should be punished for publishing the paper, which he called “highly partisan.”

“It’s, I think, the worst paper I’ve ever seen in the history of the Federal Reserve system,” Hassett told CNBC. “The people associated with this paper should presumably be disciplined.”

The Supreme Court is also set to rule on whether the president has the authority to impose such widespread tariffs before its term ends this summer. Trump has cited the International Emergency Economic Powers Act, which allows the president to regulate economic transactions in the event of a national emergency, in imposing the import taxes.

data.title: Gates cancels high-profile AI speech amid Epstein backlash
data.description:
Microsoft co-founder Bill Gates canceled a speech at India’s AI Impact Summit on Thursday amid an ongoing backlash over his ties to disgraced financier Jeffrey Epstein.

“After careful consideration and to ensure the focus remained on the AI Summit’s key priorities, Mr. Gates did not deliver the keynote address at the AI Summit,” the Gates Foundation said in a statement.

The organization said it was instead represented at the summit by Ankur Vora, president of the foundation’s Africa and India offices. The gathering has drawn many of the world’s top tech leaders.

“The Gates Foundation remains fully committed to our work in India to advance our shared health and development goals,” it added.

Gates’s ties to Epstein, which were previously known, came under scrutiny again when the Justice Department released a new tranche of emails from Epstein in late January.

A spokesperson called the unverified allegations included in two emails Epstein sent to himself about Gates in 2013 “absolutely absurd and completely false,” arguing that the “only thing these documents demonstrate is Epstein’s frustration that he did not have an ongoing relationship with Gates and the lengths he would go to entrap and defame.”

However, Gates later apologized for his ties to Epstein, saying he was “foolish to spend time with him.”

“Every minute I spent with him, I regret, and I apologize that I did that,” he said in an interview with Australia’s Nine News published earlier this month.

“I was one of many people who regret ever knowing him,” he added. “The more that comes out, the more clear it will be that, although the time was a mistake, it has nothing to do with that kind of behaviour﻿.”

Amid the uproar, House Oversight and Government Reform Committee Chair James Comer (R-Ky.) suggested that his panel would seek more information from Gates as part of its probe into Epstein and that it is “highly likely” they will subpoena the billionaire if he refuses to cooperate.

data.title: Judge throws out ruling backing Trump mass detention policy
data.description:
A federal judge on Wednesday vacated an immigration court ruling giving the Trump administration board powers to detain migrants, forcing them to give bond hearings and then possibly release thousands in custody.

The ruling from U.S. District Court Judge Sunshine Sykes excoriated Trump administration claims they are targeting the worst of the worst for deportation.

The Department of Homeland Security (DHS) and U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) are at times relying on shaky legal ground to detain migrants, wrote Sykes, an appointeee of former President Biden, and migrants are therefore entitled to a bond hearing to determine whether they may pursue their immigration case outside of ICE detention.

“‘Worst of the worst’ is an inaccurate description of most of those affected by DHS and ICE’s operations. Perhaps in utilizing this extreme language DHS seeks to justify the magnitude and scope of its operations against non-criminal noncitizens. Maybe that phrase merely mirrors the severity and ill-natured conduct by the Government. Even though these press releases might contain an inkling of truth, they ignore a greater, more dire reality,” she wrote.

“It is not the ‘worst of the worst’ that are swept into the nationwide and reckless violations of the law by the executive branch,” she said, pointing to the cause of Liam Conejo Ramos, who with his father were detained “without a valid warrant.”

The decision comes after Sykes previously determined in December the wide-ranging detention program was illegal, but determined those being held merited greater relief from the court. 

Migrants are typically only denied bond hearings before an immigration court judge when they are considered recent arrivals.

But the Trump administration bucked decades of precedent in determining those arrested by ICE after spending years in the U.S. should also be denied a bond hearing.

Sykes said despite her earlier ruling, migrants were continually being denied bond hearings, and were instead forced to file habeas corpus petitions to seek their release.

“As evidenced by the volume of habeas petitions filed by Bond Eligible Class members and the conduct of Respondents in continuing to violate the rights of those class members, further relief is necessary,” she wrote.

Sykes’s ruling, however, clashes with a decision last week by the 5th Circuit Court of Appeals, which split 2-1 in determining their detention practices were legal.

“After reviewing carefully the relevant provisions and structure of the Immigration and Naturalization Act, the statutory history, and Congressional intent, we conclude that the government’s position is correct,” the majority opinion concluded.

data.title: Watch live: Trump to tout economic agenda in remarks from Georgia
data.description:
President Trump will speak in Rome, Ga., on Thursday afternoon — the latest stop in a domestic tour to sell his economic agenda ahead of November’s midterm elections.

Trump has consistently praised his administration’s efforts to address affordability and inflation concerns, despite polling showing voters are worried about rising costs and potential impacts from sweeping tariffs.

His visit to Georgia follows similar trips to Iowa and Michigan earlier this year.

It also comes after the FBI raided an election office in Fulton County, Ga., last month and seized records related to the 2020 presidential election. Trump has maintained, without evidence, that the election was stolen.

The president, ahead of his speech, is also expected to visit a local business and a factory.

The event is scheduled to begin at 4 p.m. EST.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: House Democrat: 'Very hard to believe' Wexner testimony
data.description:
Rep. Yassamin Ansari (D-Ariz.) cast doubt on the credibility of billionaire Les Wexner, who testified before the House Oversight Committee Wednesday about his ties to convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein. 

Ansari, a member of the committee, told host Kaitlan Collins on CNN’s “The Source” that she went into the deposition with an “earnest attempt to ask questions and was hoping to get as many substantive answers as possible” from Wexner. 

Epstein was Wexner’s financial manager for roughly two decades, although the businessman denied knowledge of the disgraced financier’s crimes on Wednesday. The founder of Bath and Body Works and former CEO of Victoria’s Secret told the panel that Epstein lived a “double life,” and he regretted knowing the “clever, diabolical … master manipulator.”

The Arizona Democrat said it was “hard to believe him” based on the questions asked and the responses given.

Wexner testified from his estate in New Albany, Ohio, with multiple Oversight Democrats traveling to the Buckeye State to attend the deposition in person. 

An FBI memo from August 2019 released by the Justice Department (DOJ) earlier this month lists Wexner as a potential Epstein co-conspirator. The memo was compiled days after the financier, who was awaiting trial on sex trafficking charges, died in prison by what authorities have ruled a suicide. 

A lawyer for Wexner told The New York Times in December that the 88-year-old was not the target of a federal investigation or considered an accomplice. Ansari on Wednesday said she was “shocked” to hear that he had not been questioned by DOJ or the FBI.

“When you think of Jeffrey Epstein, you think of Les Wexner,” the lawmaker said. “There’s no name that I have heard more from the victims, after I hear about Ghislaine [Maxwell] and Jeffrey himself, than Les Wexner, because they say, ‘Follow the money.'”

Ansari also told CNN that when she asked Wexner whether he was friends with Epstein, the businessman replied, “No.” She added that the ex-financial manager could not recall why he trusted Epstein with his money. 

“Then we‘d ask him questions about, why he trusted [Epstein] with so much money, despite it being reported that many of his close advisers at the time advised against it,” she continued. “He said he didn‘t remember.”

The lawmaker added, “So because of that very basic question, not receiving a response that I felt was believable, it was hard to trust everything else that was brought up during today‘s deposition.”

data.title: Trump needs a fiscal sustainability reset
data.description:
When President Trump won a second term one of his first instincts was to address the need to reduce fraud, waste, abuse and mismanagement in the federal government. On Nov. 12, 2024, just one week after winning, he wrote that a new Department of Government Efficiency “will become, potentially, ‘The Manhattan Project’ of our time.”

He correctly noted that “Republican politicians have dreamed about the objectives of ‘DOGE’ for a very long time.” He said the department would “liberate our Economy” and make government accountable to “WE THE PEOPLE.” It would make “life better for all Americans,” he promised, and when it completed its work on July 4, 2026, it would be “the perfect gift to America on the 250th Anniversary of The Declaration of Independence.”

We hoped the president’s lofty goals for DOGE would be achieved, but we were skeptical. The department demonstrated the inadequacy of the federal government’s internal controls and information systems, but it fell far short of its financial goals. We estimate that DOGE actually achieved about $20-$30 billion of its the more than $200 billion in cuts that it claimed.

Bringing disruptive outside voices to government is generally a wise strategy but how you conduct your mission matters. The late Sen. Tom Coburn (R-Okla.), with whom we worked, was an authentic outsider who helped generate far more savings than DOGE. His partnership with the Government Accountability Office to eliminate duplication, overlap and fragmentation alone has saved an accumulated $725 billion, according to GAO’s most recent estimate.

Fortunately, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has been persuading Trump that it is more effective to “move deliberately and fix things” than to move fast and break things. In doing so, he is essentially channeling Coburn. 

President Trump’s instincts were right in November 2024. He still has time to pivot to a DOGE 2.0 agenda by emphasizing five priorities.

First, direct the White House Office of Management and Budget to lead a 21st Century Federalism Transformation Initiative to renew limited, constitutional government. Today’s bloated administrative state features more than 400 agencies and subagencies when our Constitution only authorizes four to five. The Trump administration, in concert with governors and outside transformation experts, can present Congress with a government reorganization plan and dare them to vote no.

Second, encourage members of Congress who are serious about reform to support House Budget Committee Chairman Jodey Arrington’s (R-Texas) reconciliation 2.0 concept. Congress can use the reconciliation rules to address fraud, which is a systemic feature of federal programs, not an isolated bug. This can generate substantial savings, particularly in big-ticket areas like health care.

Third, support a long overdue upgrade to USASpending.gov — the website created in 2006 by then-Sens. Coburn and Barack Obama (D-Ill.) — that will require real-time transparency and better tracking of subaward spending. Congress and the president can lead by example at the federal level and encourage states to do the same, which would create a powerful check on fraudsters in states like Minnesota.

Fourth, support the formation of a statutory fiscal commission like the one spelled out in the  Fiscal Commission Act. Some may assert we already have a standing fiscal commission — namely, Congress. But federal elected officials have persistently failed to address the huge deficits and mounting debt burdens that threaten our collective future. Congress is highly unlikely to do so on a piecemeal basis through regular order. 

Trump has so far not shown a serious appetite to reform our unsustainable social insurance programs, which are the primary drivers of our debt and deficits. But he should tell the public the truth: Significant cuts to Social Security and Medicare will occur if these programs are not reformed. In addition, failure to defuse our ticking debt bomb will have serious adverse economic, national security, and domestic consequences over time.

Fifth, support efforts to force Congress to call a limited Article Five Convention to propose a fiscal responsibility amendment. The Federal Fiscal Sustainability Foundation and the National Federalism Commission have provided conclusive evidence that the Congress should have done so as far back as 1979. Since then, total federal debt has risen from $1 trillion to more than $38 trillion. In addition, the dollar has lost over 80 percent of its value while our total debt-to-GDP ratio has gone from 33 percent to 125 percent.

When presidents win elections, they get to choose their signature issues. But when their signatures issues become misaligned with the electorate’s issues, their party pays a price.

Fiscal issues affect all Americans. If the president renews his focus on fiscal sustainability, his success will be our success, and his legacy will benefit accordingly.

David Walker served as the comptroller general of the United States under Presidents Bill Clinton and George W. Bush. John Hart is CEO of Open the Books and co-author of “The Debt Bomb” with U.S. Senator Tom Coburn.

data.title: 100+ Oklahoma students suspended after walkout
data.description:
Oklahoma Gov. Kevin Stitt (R) on Wednesday celebrated the suspension of more than 100 students at Mustang Public Schools who walked out of class to protest U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE). 

“I applaud Mustang Superintendent Dr. Charles Bradley for suspending 122 students who walked out of class to protest,” the governor wrote on X. “Young Oklahomans: Free speech is sacred, but truancy robs your future. Stay in school, build skills, and make your voice heard responsibly.” 

The school district said it told students they were expected to stay in class after discovering plans for a classroom walkout, according to a statement received by NewsNation, sister-station of The Hill.  

Mustang Public Schools said students who chose to ignore the warning and participate in the walkout got in-school suspension for unexcused absences. Classes went on as normal for students who did not participate.  

“Despite social media claims alleging adult involvement, district leadership reviewed all allegations and found no evidence that any Mustang Public Schools employee instigated, organized, or facilitated this student activity. Individuals identified in online posts as district employees were either not affiliated with Mustang Public Schools or were inaccurately represented,” the statement reads.  

The Hill has reached out to the district for comment.  

“Anti-ICE” classroom walkouts have popped up around the country after two protesters were killed by federal immigration officers in Minneapolis. 

Due to the disruptive nature of a classroom walkout, experts say it is not one of the forms of protests protected for students by the First Amendment.  

The protests are putting schools under a spotlight with Texas and Florida threatening consequences for K-12 districts they suspect encouraged the students’ actions.

data.title: Virginia Giuffre family on Andrew's arrest: 'Broken hearts have been lifted'
data.description:
The family of Virginia Giuffre, who alleged that Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor sexually abused her after she was trafficked by Jeffrey Epstein, wrote Thursday that their “broken hearts have been lifted” by news of the former prince’s arrest.

“At last. Today, our broken hearts have been lifted at the news that no one is above the law, not even royalty,” the family wrote in a statement obtained by The Hill’s sister network NewsNation, offering their “gratitude” to the Thames Valley Police.

“He was never a prince. For survivors everywhere, Virginia did this for you,” the statement added.  

Mountbatten-Windsor was reportedly taken into custody around 8 a.m. local time Thursday at the Sandringham Estate in Norfolk on suspicion of misconduct in public office, according to the BBC.

The arrest came as Mountbatten-Windsor faces fresh scrutiny over his ties to Epstein following the Justice Department’s latest release of files connected to the convicted sex offender and disgraced financier.

The BBC reported that the arrest likely stems from allegations around his activities while serving as a trade envoy for the United Kingdom. The Epstein files showed him sharing confidential government information, according to the outlet, which noted the specifics of the police investigation have not yet been made public.

Mountbatten-Windsor has also been accused of wrongdoing in connection with Giuffre, who wrote in a posthumously published memoir that she suffered abuse at the hands of the former royal after being trafficked to him by Epstein and his associate Ghislaine Maxwell.  

Giuffre, who was 17 at the time of the alleged abuse, described him in the book as “entitled” and said he acted “as if he believed having sex with me was his birthright.”

Mountbatten-Windsor has denied the allegations and said he never met her. He did, however, settle a lawsuit brought by Giuffre in 2022, though the financial terms were not publicized.

Giuffre also claimed that Mountbatten-Windsor used the royal pulpit to smear her once allegations became public, saying he hired “internet trolls to hassle” her.

King Charles III said in a statement Thursday following his brother’s arrest that “the law must take its course.”

“What now follows is the full, fair and proper process by which this issue is investigated in the appropriate manner and by the appropriate authorities. In this, as I have said before, they have our full and wholehearted support and co-operation,” Charles added.  

Giuffre died by suicide last April at the age of 41. Her family has since been pushing for passage of an eponymous law aimed at eliminating the statute of limitations for federal sex trafficking crimes in the U.S., a change she advocated for in her memoir.

“Let this be the moment this country chooses accountability over denial, courage over comfort, and justice over obstacles,” Giuffre’s sister-in-law, Amanda Roberts, said during a press conference earlier this month.

data.title: Democratic lawmaker: 'If a Prince can be held accountable, so can a President'
data.description:
Democratic lawmakers reacted to the news of former Prince Andrew’s reported arrest on Thursday, with Rep. Melanie Stansbury (D-N.M.) suggesting President Trump should also be subject to investigation over his ties to convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.

“If a Prince can be held accountable, so can a President,” Stansbury posted on social platform X, without naming Trump.

Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor was taken into custody early Thursday morning on “suspicion of misconduct while in public office,” according to a statement from Thames Valley Police. 

The arrest follows the Justice Department’s (DOJ) release of files related to Epstein, which revealed that the brother of King Charles III shared confidential information with the disgraced financier during his time as a British trade envoy. Mountbatten-Windsor was stripped of his royal title last year and moved out of his royal residence over his ties to Epstein.

Democrats have long accused Trump of shielding Epstein’s crimes, noting professional and social links between the two men. The president has denied any knowledge or involvement in Epstein’s illicit activities, and he has not been publicly accused by survivors of Epstein’s crimes. 

House Oversight Democrats also responded to Andrew’s arrest in a post on X. 

“No one connected to Jeffrey Epstein will escape accountability for their crimes,” the group wrote. “Our work is just beginning, and we will pursue justice for every individual who participated in the abuse of women and girls.”

The House Oversight and Government Reform Committee has led an investigation into Epstein and documents tied to his sex trafficking case — including the files released by DOJ under the Epstein Files Transparency Act. Members have also sought to interview prominent figures named in the trove, including Mountbatten-Windsor. 

Former President Clinton and former Secretary of State Hillary Clinton are set to testify before the committee next week about the extent of their knowledge into Epstein’s illicit activities. 

Rep. Suhas Subramanyam (D-Va.), a member of the Oversight Committee, commended police for arresting the former prince in a post online. 

“Andrew Mountbatten Windsor has evaded justice for too long,” Subramanyam wrote. “We will continue to call on him to cooperate in our investigation, and I hope today’s arrest will lead to answers.”

Former Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene (G-Ga.) has joined Democrats in criticizing the Trump administration’s handling of the investigation into Epstein. She previously called the investigation the “biggest political miscalculation” of the president’s career. 

“The UK has arrested Andrew because of the Epstein files and over here the President signed an EO protecting cancer causing Glyphosate in our foods and we have zero Epstein related arrest and investigations since release of the files,” the former lawmaker wrote Thursday, following the arrest.

“And we are on the verge of going to war against Iran,” she continued. “That’s the current state of MAGA and MAHA.”

King Charles also weighed in on the news, saying “the law must take its course.”
